summary: Jeff Atwood shares an ASP.NET CAPTCHA server control he created by converting and repackaging existing code into a drag-and-drop component. The implementation requires minimal setup with only three properties to configure, though it does need an HttpHandler entry in web.config. He tested the CAPTCHA against professional OCR software and found it effective due to its low contrast and warped characters. The post serves as a brief technical announcement pointing readers to the full CodeProject article.
key_points: Converted an existing CAPTCHA code sample to VB.NET and packaged it as a reusable ASP.NET server control
The control is designed for simplicity with only three properties to configure
Requires an HttpHandler configuration in web.config to serve dynamically generated images
Tested against OmniPage Pro 14 OCR software and proved resistant to simple OCR attacks
Low contrast and character warping are the key features that make the CAPTCHA effective
money_quotes: It's as simple as I could make it: a total drag and drop, set the (three) properties and forget it implementation.
I tested this CAPTCHA against OCR software, specifically OmniPage Pro 14.
It does surprisingly well for a couple key reasons – low contrast, and all the characters are warped.
It's possible to defeat it, but it's definitely not a trivial 'drag to OCR window' situation.
key_insight: Effective CAPTCHAs don't need to be complex—simple techniques like low contrast and character warping can provide meaningful protection against automated OCR attacks.
